source_label: Nic Carter — How Much Energy Does Bitcoin Actually Consume? (Harvard Business Review)
excerpt: Per-transaction energy figures conflate two different things: the energy used to extend the chain and the number of transactions any given block happens to contain. Energy is spent securing blocks, not transactions; Lightning, batched exchange withdrawals, and any future second-layer activity all consume effectively zero additional mainchain energy.
body:
Source excerpt:
Per-transaction energy figures conflate two different things: the energy used to extend the chain and the number of transactions any given block happens to contain. Energy is spent securing blocks, not transactions; Lightning, batched exchange withdrawals, and any future second-layer activity all consume effectively zero additional mainchain energy.

---

Cited by leaf "Energy use in context":
Bitcoin mining uses real energy on purpose — that's the point of proof of work, not a side effect. The industry-wide draw sits in the rough order of 150–180 TWh/year depending on the price-driven hashrate at any given moment, which is on the order of 0.5% of global electricity production and well below comparable industries like residential air conditioning, data centers in aggregate, or refining gold [1]. Whether that's a lot or a little depends entirely on what you think the resulting settlement layer is worth, which is not a question energy data can answer.

The interesting structural fact is that Bitcoin miners are uniquely good at consuming energy that nobody else can use. Mining hardware is location-agnostic, modular, interruptible on a minute's notice, and pays in a globally-traded asset, which makes it the natural buyer for stranded hydro, flared methane that would otherwise be vented, curtailed wind and solar, and isolated geothermal [2]. The grid economics show up clearly: Texas ERCOT pays large miners to switch off during peaks (demand response), miners are paid by oil producers to flare-capture rather than vent, and Bhutan, Paraguay, Ethiopia, and El Salvador are all monetizing surplus generation that has no transmission path to a population center.

Numbers to read carefully: "X kWh per transaction" is a misleading metric — energy goes into securing blocks, not transactions, and a block's transaction count is a function of demand and Lightning offload, not energy [3]. Marginal energy per Lightning payment is approximately zero. Aggregate consumption tracks Bitcoin's price (because price drives hashrate) more than it tracks any single user behavior. The honest framing is: this much energy buys this much immutability of this much settled value, and you're free to evaluate the tradeoff on the merits.
